knowledge:
• Fuel: Outback stations 200–500km+ apart. “Last fuel” signs are serious — they mean it. Remote stations: EFTPOS sometimes cash-only, sometimes card-only after hours. Fuel is $0.40–1.00/L more in remote areas. Always fill up when you see a servo in the outback — never assume the next one is open. EV charging is genuinely sparse outside major cities and coastal highways.
• Road trains: 53.5m, up to 4 trailers, 130+ tonnes. They cannot stop quickly. Never overtake unless clear road for 1.5km+. Pull hard left, stop, let them pass. The draft will rock your car violently. Flash your lights to let them know you’re there at night.
• Bakeries: THE quintessential Aussie road stop. Country bakeries are cultural institutions — often family-run for generations. Pies (pepper steak, mince, curry, lamb), sausage rolls, vanilla slices (“snot blocks”), lamingtons, scrolls. The best intel in any town comes from the bakery counter. Arrive before noon for the best selection. A country bakery pie and a farmer’s union iced coffee is the definitive road trip meal.
• Pubs: Even the tiniest towns have a pub. Counter meals ($18–30) are often genuinely excellent — parma, steak, fish & chips, roast of the day. The pub is always the first place to ask for local knowledge: “what’s worth seeing around here?” gets better answers than Google. Cold beer on a hot day after 300km of driving is one of life’s great pleasures. Most pubs have accommodation (“pub rooms”) for $80–120/night — basic but clean, often the only option in small towns.
• Wildlife: Kangaroos, wombats, echidnas worst at dawn/dusk/night. Hitting a roo at 110km/h totals a car and can seriously injure occupants. Cattle grids = livestock area. Emus run sideways and are completely unpredictable. Wedge-tailed eagles on roadkill will take off directly at your windscreen. In the tropics: watch for cassowaries (endangered, large, dangerous) and crocodiles near any water north of Rockhampton.
• Free camps: Often the best spots in Australia — riverside, coastal, rainforest clearings. WikiCamps app is essential ($8, worth every cent). Camps Australia Wide and Campermate are good alternatives. 72hr limits common. Leave no trace — the community depends on good behaviour and councils will close camps that get trashed. Many free camps have drop toilets and fire rings. Check fire bans before lighting.
• Swimming holes: Australia’s greatest hidden treasure. Gorge pools, waterfalls, river bends, tidal rock pools. Often unmarked or known only locally. Always check depth before jumping. In the tropics (north of Tropic of Capricorn): NEVER swim in rivers, estuaries, or near mangroves — saltwater crocodiles are real and lethal. Look for “no swimming — crocodiles” signs and BELIEVE them. South of the tropics, freshwater rivers and gorges are generally safe.
• Water: Town water safe everywhere. Outback bore water: safe but mineral-heavy (tastes different). NEVER drink from rivers/creeks without purifying — giardia is endemic. Carry at least 5L per person per day in remote areas. In summer outback, 10L minimum.
• Time zones: QLD no daylight saving. NSW/VIC/TAS/ACT do (Oct–Apr). SA is UTC+9:30. NT stays CST year-round. WA is 2–3hrs behind east coast. Broken Hill uses SA time despite being in NSW. Border towns can be confusing.
• Gravel roads: 80km/h max for most vehicles, 60km/h for caravans. Stones chip paint and crack windscreens (budget $200–400 for windscreen replacement on long outback trips). Pull fully left when vehicles pass. Dust from road trains can blind you for 30+ seconds — pull over, stop, and wait for it to clear.
• Mobile coverage: Falls to zero between towns in rural Australia. Telstra has the best rural coverage by far (Optus and Vodafone are city-only outside the coast). Download offline maps before leaving range. Consider a Telstra prepaid SIM if on another network. Satellite phones or PLBs for truly remote areas (Nullarbor, Cape York, Simpson Desert, Tanami).
• Emergency: 000 is universal. PLB/EPIRB for remote areas (rent from RFDS offices, outdoor shops). Royal Flying Doctor Service covers the entire outback. HF radio at roadhouses can relay emergency messages. If your car breaks down in remote Australia: STAY WITH THE VEHICLE. It’s easier to find a car than a person walking.
• Indigenous communities: Alcohol restrictions are common and legally enforced. Entry permits required for some areas (parts of Cape York, Arnhem Land, APY Lands in SA, some WA communities). No photography of people or ceremonies without explicit permission. Support indigenous-owned businesses and tours — they offer genuine cultural experiences. Sacred sites should be treated with respect: don’t climb, don’t remove anything.
• Roadhouses: The lifeblood of outback Australia. Part servo, part cafe, part motel, part general store, part mechanic, part post office. Prices are high (burger and chips $22–28) but they’re literally keeping you alive out there. Most have basic accommodation ($100–160/night). Some are famous destinations in their own right (Birdsville Hotel, William Creek, Daly Waters Pub).
• Weather extremes: Outback summer (Dec–Feb) regularly hits 42–48°C. Car air conditioning struggles. Carry extra coolant. Bitumen can melt and tyres blow more easily. Start driving at dawn, rest midday, continue in the afternoon. In winter: southern highlands and Tassie get frost and occasional snow. Black ice on bridges at dawn. NT/QLD wet season (Nov–Apr): roads flood fast and stay closed for days/weeks.
• Camping etiquette: Generator hours are typically 8am–8pm. Dogs must be on leash in national parks (check rules — some parks ban dogs entirely). Fire bans are TOTAL — no campfires, no fuel stoves in some states. Grey water should be disposed of properly, not dumped on the ground. Pack out everything. Leave your campsite cleaner than you found it.
